Empires & Puzzles Hisan Review: 3-Star Green Sniper Guide

Hisan might look like a majestic golden steed, but his stats scream 'glass cannon.' Discover why this Sand Empire veteran is still a lethal sniper in 3-star tournaments despite having the durability of a soap bubble.
Hisan Review: The Glass Cannon Stallion with a Healing Block Kick
Hisan, The Glass Cannon Stallion: Fast Sniper, Healing Blocker, Fragile Bones
Hisan is the trusty steed of the Sand Empire family, a

who has been galloping through Empires & Puzzles since the early days of the game. Originally released as part of the seasonal Sand Empire event, he quickly established himself as a premier 3-star sniper.

While modern "power creep" has left his defensive stats in the dust, Hisan remains a relevant tactical nuke in specific tournament formats. He doesn't have a costume, so what you see is what you get: a fast, hard-hitting horse who absolutely refuses to let his enemies visit the doctor.

Visually, Hisan is a stunning golden Arabian horse, captured in a dynamic rearing pose against a backdrop of desert dunes and minarets. It’s a clean, classic design that stands out because, well, he's just a horse. No rider, no armor, just a very angry, magical equine ready to trample you. It’s refreshing to see a hero that is literally just an animal doing its best.
Hisan's stat distribution is the definition of "extreme." He pushes all his chips into Attack and leaves his Defense at the door.
When we compare Hisan to modern 3-star heroes, the age gap shows significantly in his survivability.

. New heroes are pushing 900-1000 HP. Hisan has nearly half the health pool of a modern tank.
He is a classic "Glass Cannon." He hits as hard as a modern hero but breaks twice as fast.
, is a multi-layered threat that offers immense utility for a 3-star hero:
280% at Fast speed is a solid sniper hit. It's enough to severely dent or kill softer 3-star targets.
The 104 damage over 4 turns adds up, often finishing off enemies who survive the initial impact.

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
family, which offers a simple but useful stat boost.

The bonus provides +10% Defense for 2 heroes and +20% for 3 heroes. Given Hisan's wet-tissue-paper defense stat, this bonus is actually quite helpful if you run him with other Sand heroes (like Arman or costumed Roc/Rana if mixing rarities in wars). However, in strict 3-star tournaments, you rarely run enough Sand heroes to make this a primary strategy.
Hisan is from an era before passives were handed out like candy. He has

is a fitting power for a glass cannon. At the start of battle, if he gets hit, his Special Skill damage increases. Since he's likely to die quickly, front-loading his damage potential is the best way to use him.
In 2018-2019, Hisan was a top-tier 3-star sniper. He was often compared to Berden and usually considered superior due to the healing block and higher attack stat.
While his Attack stat has aged surprisingly well (still top 10% for base 3-stars), his bulk has completely evaporated. He is a relic of a time when 600 HP was considered "healthy."
Hisan is no longer a general-purpose hero. You cannot put him on defense; he will die to a stiff breeze (ironic, given his skill name). However, on
tournaments, his speed and healing block make him a tactical scalpel. He exists to delete one key target before expiring.
With Hisan, you are fighting a losing battle trying to make him tanky. His base Defense and HP are so low that percentage-based emblem boosts yield poor returns. Lean into his strength.
Maximizes his tile damage and sniper hit. Ensures that when he fires, he kills.
Even with full defense emblems, he won't survive a sniper hit from a modern hero. Better to ensure he takes someone down with him.
path is recommended. Treat him as a disposable missile.
Excellent tile damage for a 3-star hero, great for stacking Green.
The -100% healing debuff completely shuts down healers and regeneration, which is invaluable against annoying tanks like Grevle or Faiez.

Unlike Berden, Belith, or Brienne, Hisan lacks a costume to update his stats and skills.
In a meta of multi-hitters, single-target damage can feel slow if it doesn't secure a kill.
His speed and high attack make him great for quick kills.
His high attack stat makes him a good tile-damage carrier for Mono Green teams.
Good for taking out a remaining healer in a damaged enemy team.
Hisan works best when protected or when used in a "Kill Fast" strategy.
Brienne drops defense, and then Berden and Hisan double-tap enemies. Hisan's high attack benefits massively from the defense down.
Use this against heavy healing teams. Hisan blocks the main heal, Tyrum strips buffs, and Kvasir prevents minion meat shields.
Don't pair him with slow, defensive heroes. He needs to win fast.

If you pull Hisan early, level him! He is a fantastic sniper for Season 1 map stages and early Rare Quests. His high tile damage will help you punch above your weight class against blue bosses. Just don't put him in the center of your defense team.
You likely have better options like

tournaments. His ability to prevent reviving (if the mechanic relies on healing) or regeneration is unique at Fast speed for Nature 3-stars. Do not Limit Break him unless you are swimming in resources and really love the horse; his stats don't scale well enough to justify the Aethers compared to modern Toons.
The community has always had a soft spot for the "Sand Horse."
Years ago, the debate was "Berden vs. Hisan." Many players preferred Hisan for the "higher attack stat" and the cleanse, noting that "Berden goes pew pew pew slowly, Hisan is fast."
Players noted early on, "I just maxed Mnesseus and this horse shows up... personally I would use 2x snipers."
As one player lamented recently, "It’s a shame that for so many years he and Arman didn’t even live to see their costumes."

Hisan is a relic of a simpler time in Empires & Puzzles, a time when a horse kicking you in the face was considered peak combat strategy. While he has been mathematically left behind by modern super-heroes, his core design—Fast, High Attack, Healing Block—remains fundamentally sound. He is a niche tool for your 3-star toolkit, a glass dagger to be unsheathed when you absolutely, positively need to stop a Grevle from healing.
A viable offensive specialist for tournaments, but keep him away from the front lines or he'll be glue before the battle starts.
